title: X-ray and Gamma Ray Production and Energy
narrationScript: Last lesson we established that X rays and gamma rays are identical photons with different birthplaces. This lesson visits both birthplaces, because how the photon is made decides everything you can and cannot control on the job. Start inside the X ray tube. The tube is an evacuated envelope with two electrodes. On one side sits the cathode, a tungsten filament much like the one in an old light bulb. Run a heating current through it and the filament glows white hot, boiling off a cloud of free electrons. That boil off is called thermionic emission, and the filament current sets how many electrons are available. On the other side sits the anode, carrying a tungsten target. Apply a high voltage between cathode and anode, tens to hundreds of kilovolts, and every free electron is snatched across the vacuum and slammed into the target at a fraction of the speed of light. Two things happen in that target. Most electrons swerve close to a tungsten nucleus and are violently decelerated, and a decelerating charge radiates energy. That radiation is braking radiation, bremsstrahlung, and because every electron loses a different amount of energy in a different encounter, the result is a continuous spectrum, photons at every energy from nearly zero up to the full tube voltage. A smaller number of electrons knock inner shell electrons clean out of tungsten atoms. When outer electrons fall into those vacancies, they emit photons at exact energies set by tungsten's shell structure. Those are characteristic X rays, sharp spikes riding on the smooth bremsstrahlung hump. Now the two knobs every radiographer must keep straight. The filament, the m a knob, controls how many electrons cross, so it controls intensity, how much radiation per second, and nothing about penetrating power. The voltage, the k v knob, controls how hard each electron hits, so it sets the maximum photon energy and shifts the whole spectrum upward, controlling penetration and the contrast of the final image. More k v, more penetrating, lower subject contrast, wider latitude. And one sobering number. Only about one percent of the electrical energy becomes X rays. The other ninety nine percent becomes heat in the target, which is why targets are tungsten backed with copper, why anodes are angled and sometimes rotated, and why machines have duty cycles and warm up schedules that you follow to avoid cracking a cold anode. Gamma ray production could not be more different. There is no machine and there are no knobs. A gamma ray is emitted by an unstable nucleus settling toward stability after a decay. Iridium one ninety two decays by beta emission, and the daughter nucleus immediately sheds its leftover energy as gamma photons. Because nuclear energy levels are exact, each nuclide emits its own fixed menu of gamma energies, a line spectrum, the same today, tomorrow, and forever. Nothing you do changes those energies or that output. No power needed, no off switch, just shielding between you and it. So learn the menus. Iridium one ninety two emits many lines that average about zero point three five M e V, useful on steel from roughly half an inch to two and a half inches. Cobalt sixty emits two high energy lines, one point one seven and one point three three M e V, suited to steel from about an inch and a half to eight inches. Selenium seventy five sits lower, with average energy around two tenths of an M e V, giving high contrast on thin wall steel where iridium would blast straight through. Output follows the menu too. A rule of thumb worth memorizing, one curie of iridium gives about half a roentgen per hour at one meter, while one curie of cobalt gives about one point three, because each cobalt decay throws two energetic gammas. X ray machines have their own energy character. A machine plated at three hundred kilovolts produces a continuous spectrum whose maximum is three hundred k e V but whose effective energy is roughly a third to half of that, so matching machines to isotopes is a matter of effective energy, not nameplate. Typical portable directional and panoramic units run from well under one hundred up to a few hundred kilovolts, covering steel to about two inches, with high energy accelerators taking over beyond that. The deep practical difference is control. An X ray machine lets you tune energy and intensity to the part, and it goes completely cold when the power is off. A gamma source is compact, rugged, needs no power, and works in a pipe rack a mile from the nearest outlet, but its energy is fixed by physics and its output fades only on the half life schedule. Choosing between them, and explaining that choice, is Level Two work, and it starts with the production physics you now have.
imagePrompt: Inside an industrial X-ray bay, a technician aligns a boxy orange cable-connected X-ray tube head toward a steel casting on a turntable, the control console with kilovoltage and milliamperage dials visible through the shielded window behind.

sections.heading: Inside the X-ray Tube
sections.body:
An X-ray tube is a vacuum envelope holding two electrodes. The **cathode** carries a tungsten **filament**: a heating current makes it glow white-hot, boiling off free electrons (**thermionic emission**). Facing it, the **anode** carries a tungsten **target**, usually set in a copper block for heat removal. Apply a high voltage - tens to hundreds of **kilovolts** - across the tube, and the electron cloud is accelerated through the vacuum and driven into the target at a large fraction of light speed.

The geometry of the impact area matters too: the electrons strike a small spot - the **focal spot** - and its effective size, reduced by angling the target face, sets the geometric unsharpness the tube can ever achieve. A smaller focal spot means sharper images but concentrates heat.

And heat is the tube's central problem: only about **1% of the electrical energy becomes X-rays; roughly 99% becomes heat** in the target. That is why anodes are angled, copper-backed, oil- or gas-cooled, sometimes rotated - and why every machine has a **warm-up schedule and duty cycle**. Following the warm-up procedure is not ritual; firing full kV into a cold anode can crack the target.

sections.caption: X-ray tube cutaway: the filament (mA) supplies the electrons, the kilovoltage (kV) slams them into the angled tungsten target, and about 99% of the energy becomes heat.

sections.heading: Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic X-rays
sections.body:
Two processes in the target convert electron energy to photons:

- **Bremsstrahlung ('braking radiation')** - most of the beam. An electron swerving close to a tungsten nucleus decelerates abruptly, and a decelerating charge radiates. Because every electron loses a different amount of energy in a different encounter, the photons form a **continuous spectrum** from near zero up to a hard limit equal to the full tube voltage (a 200 kV tube emits nothing above 200 keV).
- **Characteristic X-rays** - a minority. Some electrons knock **inner-shell electrons** out of target atoms; when outer electrons drop into the vacancies, they emit photons at **exact energies fixed by the target element's shell structure**. These appear as sharp spikes riding on the bremsstrahlung hump (for tungsten, the K-lines near 59 and 67 keV).

The two operator controls map cleanly onto this physics. **Milliamperage (mA)** sets the filament's electron supply: more electrons, more photons - **intensity only**. **Kilovoltage (kV)** sets how hard each electron hits: it raises the spectrum's maximum and shifts its bulk upward - **penetrating quality**, and with it the contrast/latitude trade you will tune in the technique lessons.

sections.heading: Gamma Ray Production: No Machine, No Knobs
sections.body:
A **gamma ray** is emitted by an unstable nucleus shedding excess energy as it settles toward stability after a decay. Take the workhorse: an **Ir-192** nucleus decays by **beta emission** (a neutron becomes a proton, ejecting an electron), leaving the daughter nucleus in an excited state. It immediately drops to its ground state by emitting **gamma photons**.

Because nuclear energy levels are exact, each nuclide emits a fixed menu of gamma energies - a **discrete line spectrum** - identical for every atom of that nuclide, forever. Compare that with the tube:

- **No energy control.** Ir-192 emits the same lines on every job; you cannot 'turn up' a gamma source. Energy is selected by **choosing the nuclide**, not by adjusting it.
- **No intensity control.** Output is set by activity, falling only on the half-life schedule.
- **No power required - and no off switch.** The source emits in the projector, in the calibration bay, and in transit. Control is shielding, distance, and time, always.

This is why gamma radiography owns remote field work - compact, rugged, powerless - while X-ray owns work that rewards tunability.

sections.heading: Energy Characteristics of the Common Sources
sections.body:
The three radioisotopes you will actually load into projectors, with the values worth memorizing:

- **Iridium-192** - half-life ≈ 74 days; many gamma lines from about 0.2 to 0.6 MeV, **average ≈ 0.35 MeV**; the workhorse for **steel roughly 0.5 to 2.5 in.**; output ≈ **0.5 R/h per Ci at 1 m**.
- **Cobalt-60** - half-life ≈ 5.27 years; two strong lines at **1.17 and 1.33 MeV**; for **steel roughly 1.5 to 8 in.**; output ≈ **1.3 R/h per Ci at 1 m** (each decay emits two energetic gammas).
- **Selenium-75** - half-life ≈ 120 days; lower average energy (≈ 0.2 MeV); **thin-wall, high-contrast** work where Ir-192 over-penetrates.

The pattern: **higher gamma energy reaches thicker steel but yields lower subject contrast**; lower energy gives the contrast that thin sections need. Matching the source energy to the thickness range is the first technique decision on any gamma job - and since the energies are fixed, the match is made in the source-ordering paperwork, not at the part.

sections.caption: Continuous X-ray spectrum with characteristic spikes versus the fixed line spectra of Ir-192 and Co-60 - the spectrum shape is the deep difference between machine and isotope.
Energy characteristics: common gamma sources versus X-ray machines.
rows: Fixed by activity; decays T½ ≈ 120 d
≈ 0.5 R/h/Ci at 1 m; decays T½ ≈ 74 d
≈ 1.3 R/h/Ci at 1 m; decays T½ ≈ 5.27 yr
Up to ≈ 2 in. (more with high-energy units)
kV sets energy, mA sets intensity; off = cold

sections.heading: Energy Characteristics of X-ray Machines
sections.body:
An X-ray machine's nameplate kilovoltage states the **maximum** photon energy, not the typical one. The continuous spectrum's **effective energy runs roughly one-third to one-half of the peak kV**, so a 300 kV unit behaves like a beam of perhaps 100-150 keV effective - not like Ir-192's 350 keV average. Comparing machines to isotopes by nameplate alone overstates the machine every time.

Working character of the families you will meet (the hardware itself is covered in module rt1-4):

- **Low-energy units (≤ ~150 kV)**: thin steel, aluminum, composites; highest contrast.
- **Portable industrial units (~150-300 kV)**: girth welds and plate to about 2 in. of steel.
- **High-energy machines (1 MeV and up, including linear accelerators)**: thick castings and vessels beyond Co-60's practical reach.

The machine's defining advantage is **adjustability**: energy tuned to the part with the kV knob, intensity and time with mA, and a beam that ceases to exist when the power is off. Its costs are power, bulk, cooling, and duty cycles. The gamma source is the mirror image: fixed energy, fading output, no power - and never, ever off.

sections.title: kV is quality, mA is quantity - and gamma has neither knob
sections.body: On a tube, **kilovoltage** sets how hard each photon can hit (penetration, contrast/latitude trade) and **milliamperage** sets how many photons per second (intensity, exposure time). A gamma source has no knobs at all: its energies are nuclear constants and its output is its activity. You select gamma 'settings' once - when you choose the nuclide.

figures.title: X-ray continuous spectrum versus gamma line spectra

interactive.title: Machine or Isotope?
interactive.intro: Classify each statement under the radiation producer it describes. Think production physics: tubes have knobs and need power; nuclei have fixed menus and no off switch.

items.label: Emits a continuous (bremsstrahlung) spectrum up to the set peak energy
items.why: Electron braking in the target loses variable energy per encounter, producing photons at every energy up to the full tube voltage.

items.label: Emits a fixed menu of discrete energy lines
items.why: Nuclear energy levels are exact, so each nuclide emits the same characteristic gamma lines forever - Co-60 at 1.17 and 1.33 MeV, for example.

items.label: Penetrating quality adjusted with a control-panel knob
items.why: Raising kilovoltage makes each electron strike harder, shifting the whole spectrum to higher energy - adjustable penetration is the tube's signature advantage.

items.label: Radiation output stops completely when de-energized
items.why: No high voltage, no accelerated electrons, no photons. A powered-down tube is cold - radioactive decay never is.

items.label: Needs no electrical power and works a mile from the nearest outlet
items.why: Decay is self-powered. Compact, rugged, power-free operation is why gamma sources own remote pipeline and rig work.

items.label: Intensity set by filament (mA) control
items.why: The filament's thermionic emission supplies the electron current; more mA means more electrons and more photons per second, with no change in energy.

items.label: Output falls on a fixed half-life schedule
items.why: Source strength is its activity, which only decays - 74 days halves an Ir-192 source no matter how it is used or stored.

items.label: About 99% of the input energy becomes heat in a tungsten target
items.why: X-ray conversion is only ~1% efficient, which is why anodes need copper backing, cooling, warm-up schedules, and duty cycles.

checkQuestions.question: In an X-ray tube, what does increasing the milliamperage (mA) do?
options: Raises the maximum photon energy
Increases the number of electrons crossing the tube, raising beam intensity without changing penetrating quality
Shrinks the focal spot for a sharper image
Raises the anode's melting point
checkQuestions.explanation: mA is the filament control: it sets how many electrons are boiled off and driven into the target, so it scales photon quantity. Energy - penetrating quality - is set by kilovoltage.

checkQuestions.question: Bremsstrahlung radiation is produced when:
options: Outer-shell electrons fill inner-shell vacancies in the target
The nucleus of the tungsten target decays
High-speed electrons decelerate sharply near target nuclei, radiating a continuous spectrum
The filament evaporates tungsten atoms
checkQuestions.explanation: A decelerating charge radiates. Each electron loses a different amount of energy in its braking encounter, so the photons span a continuum up to the full tube voltage. Characteristic X-rays - the other process - come from shell vacancies.

checkQuestions.question: Why can't a radiographer adjust the energy of an Ir-192 source for a thinner weld?
options: The projector's collimator blocks the adjustment
Gamma energies are fixed by the nuclide's nuclear energy levels; energy is selected only by choosing a different nuclide such as Se-75
Regulations forbid energy adjustment in the field
The energy could be adjusted, but only with the source fully exposed
checkQuestions.explanation: Gamma lines are nuclear constants - every Ir-192 atom emits the same menu, about 0.35 MeV average. For a thin, high-contrast job the fix is a lower-energy nuclide like Se-75, not a knob.

checkQuestions.question: Which pairing of source to steel thickness range is correct?
options: Ir-192: roughly 0.5 to 2.5 in.
Se-75: 4 to 8 in.
Ir-192: 6 to 10 in.
checkQuestions.explanation: Ir-192's ~0.35 MeV average suits steel from about 0.5 to 2.5 in. Co-60 (1.17/1.33 MeV) covers roughly 1.5 to 8 in., and low-energy Se-75 handles thin-wall, high-contrast work.

checkQuestions.question: A 300 kV X-ray machine and an Ir-192 source are compared. Which statement is correct?
options: The machine's beam is entirely 300 keV photons, harder than Ir-192's average
The machine's continuous spectrum has an effective energy of roughly one-third to one-half of 300 keV, so it is softer than Ir-192's 0.35 MeV average
Both emit identical discrete line spectra
The Ir-192 source can be tuned down to match the machine
checkQuestions.explanation: 300 kV is only the spectrum's hard limit; the bremsstrahlung bulk sits far lower, giving an effective energy near 100-150 keV. Nameplate kV always overstates a machine compared against an isotope's average energy.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 4.3 (X-ray production), 4.4 (gamma ray production)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 4.5 (gamma ray energy), 4.6 (energy characteristics of common radioisotope sources), 4.7 (energy characteristics of X-ray machines)
Source values: Ir-192 ≈ 0.35 MeV avg / 0.5 R/h/Ci at 1 m; Co-60 1.17 + 1.33 MeV / 1.3 R/h/Ci at 1 m; Se-75 ≈ 0.2 MeV avg (reference values)
